lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — The HSCT-gene-therapy convergence
I want to step back and look at the structural picture, because the convergence between HSCT and gene therapy is one of the defining patterns of modern medicine, and it is hiding in plain sight.
Every approved gene therapy for a blood disease — Casgevy, Zynteglo, Skysona, Lyfgenia, Strimvelis — is autologous HSCT plus an editing or transduction step.

Every approved CAR-T therapy — Kymriah, Yescarta, Tecartus, Breyanzi, Abecma, Carvykti — is autologous lymphocyte therapy that uses the same apheresis, manufacturing, and infusion infrastructure built for transplant.
The new categories of cellular medicine are not separate from HSCT.
They are HSCT with new payloads.
This matters for hospitals, for regulators, for patients, for economics.

The institutions equipped to deliver HSCT — transplant centers with apheresis units, cell processing facilities, isolation wards, conditioning protocols, and long-term follow-up infrastructure — are the same institutions equipped to deliver gene therapy and CAR-T.
The regulatory framework — the FDA's Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research, the BLA pathway for cellular products — is the same.
The training of clinicians, nurses, pharmacists is shared.
The reimbursement battles are similar.
What this convergence means, in practice, is that the HSCT infrastructure is the delivery system for a whole emerging class of medicine.
If you want a cell-based therapy for a disease, you ask: can you collect the right cells, modify them appropriately, condition the patient to accept them, and infuse them safely?
The answer is increasingly yes for blood, immune system, and lymphoid disease.
It is increasingly being attempted for autoimmune disease, for muscular dystrophy, for inherited liver disease.
The chassis is being adapted to new payloads at a rapid rate.
The 2020s and 2030s will be the decade in which this convergence plays out.
The number of gene therapies running on HSCT infrastructure will grow.
The conditioning technology will improve.
The cost will, eventually, come down — although not soon.
The access will expand.
The procedure that E.
Donnall Thomas pioneered in Seattle in the 1960s, originally for refractory leukemia, has become the foundation for an entire generation of cures.
